question.text: A company listed on BSE has 1 crore total shares outstanding. In the preceding 12 months, 12 lakh shares were traded. An acquirer triggers an open offer. How should the offer price be determined?

answer.text: Using the VWAP of 60 trading days, as the shares are frequently traded (12% turnover exceeds the 10% threshold)
Through independent valuation, as the shares are infrequently traded
At the book value per share as determined by the latest audited financials
At the higher of face value or VWAP of 30 trading days

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Shares are "frequently traded" if turnover in the preceding 12 months is at least 10% of total shares. Here, 12 lakh out of 1 crore = 12%, which exceeds the 10% threshold. Therefore, the VWAP of 60 trading days preceding the relevant date is used for pricing. **The Trap:** 12% is close to the 10% threshold, so candidates may hesitate, but 12% clearly qualifies as frequently traded. Independent valuation would apply only if turnover were below 10%. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX (Merchant Banking) tests the frequently/infrequently traded classification. Series XV (Research Analyst) covers how trading liquidity affects valuation approaches. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The 10% turnover test is applied to the 12 months preceding the relevant date — a stock can shift from frequently traded to infrequently traded (and vice versa) depending on the measurement window.
